speaker: interviewer
text: --with Rabbi Leslie Hardman. And we are in London, England. And this is tape number four.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it appears that my name, my word got around. And it came to the notice of the authorities of the Simon Wiesenthal Center in Los Angeles. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And 10 years ago, where are we? 1995, 1940--

speaker: interviewee
text: '85. '85. They asked me to come over. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I spoke. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I was their guest, nothing else. I met a number of survivors there from Belsen. Didn't recognize them, of course. There was one Friday morning-- I was sitting in my office. And a man comes in. And he's bloated as if he'd been suffering from typhus.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was where? In your office in Belsen?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Belsen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he says, rabbi, can you help me? [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. Can you give me something in the honor of Shabbos? I said, well, are you hungry? I thought that everything had been straightened out. Oh, yes, we're not-- I'm not hungry, but it's the first time in five years I've had-- I'm really [INAUDIBLE] Shabbos. And I'd like something in honor of Shabbos.

speaker: interviewer
text: I go to my cupboard, and I find a number of tins of sardines. I'm just about to bring one down. He says, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. Wait, wait. What's the matter? He sees my tefillin. I had my tefillin there. So I brought them down, and he hug-- and he was shaking with emotion. He said, I haven't put them on for five years. I said, gesundheit. So I gave him a sitter, and he put them on. I couldn't stand it. I rushed out. I let him daven on his own. I thought, [? this. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyhow, he came back-- I came back. He'd just finished. He wrapped them up, put them in the plush bag, and he walked out. He'd forgotten the sardines. I rushed out and put it in his pocket. Said, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] Shabbos. I met him 10 years ago in-- in-- in Simon Wiesenthal Center in Los Angeles. Remarkable. Remarkable.

speaker: interviewee
text: So anyhow, so I spoke there. I was their guest. And then last year, they-- just this year, '55, 1995, they asked me to come. They'd like to honor me. When I had that they're-- they're associating me with Wallenberg, with-- with [INAUDIBLE], the Japanese [INAUDIBLE] in Lithuania, and that young girl, [? Robitta-- ?] [? Robitta ?] somebody, a-- a-- a Yiddish girl-- she was in the concentration camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: She was a very strong girl, so they sent her to munitions. And she stole a-- pieces of dynamite. And they blew up two crematoria in the concentration camp. And 300 girls escaped. All of them were captured or shot. They found out she was the ringleader. She wouldn't give any information away. And they hanged her in front of everybody. And they honored her and a doctor, William Perl, of Austria. What a-- what courage that man had. He goes to Eichmann. He says, give me-- let me-- give me 200 or 2,000 Jews. Let me take them to Palestine. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He agreed on the Monday. On the Wednesday, he changed his mind. He says, Palestine is too holy for this muck. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] He went to Berlin over his head. And he got permission. And they honored him. He was suffering from Parkinson's disease, nebbish. His wife was there. And so-- so they honored us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then suddenly, I get this. There-- there was a dinner for 1,200 people. They usually collect $2 million. After my story-- and I was shown on-- on-- on the film. One-- it must have been an organization or one man. I offer another million. And another said, half a million. So $3 and 1/2 million. So they could have made these out of gold, couldn't they? [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: But that's how I got it. It was very great-- very nice, very moving.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there many people honored that evening?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the six of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, the six of you.

speaker: interviewee
text: The six of them. Yes, the six of them. It's all-- it's all written. So I mean, the people that's in-- in-- in Los Angeles or in America will know it all. You can go-- they can collect it and put it together. Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we're coming back to this. Yes. We're going on. And time comes, time comes. And married my children. I have seven-- seven-- seven grandchildren. And-- so then we got six great-grandchildren. One more is expected in December. I'm not quite sure-- not that I've got all the money for all these things. But usually, go Pesach time to the kids in Israel. 'Cause if it's a girl-- I know I'm-- I'm-- I'm being naughty. But why schlep all that away for them? If it's a boy, you go to a bris. There's something associated with it. But I've got a sneaking feeling my elder daughter-- she's a grandma herself. Whether it's a girl or a boy, you'll come. So we'll see. Mrs. Hardman's not too well. She had both hip replacements and so on. And-- so we'll see.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said to my children, I said, apart from the money, we're getting older. There's the question of traveling. So we'll see how it goes. And that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, before we finish--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --is there some statement or something you would like to say for future generations who may view this?

speaker: interviewee
text: As long as there's a survivor left in this world, on this planet, there must be-- there must not be any forgetfulness. We had a-- something similar but not as serious or as horrific-- the Spanish Inquisition. We read about it. Occasionally, we talk about it. But it's more or less forgotten. That kind of forgetfulness can only apply to this present era of the Holocaust after every survivor is gone. And-- and that sufficient-- sufficient pressure-- or appeals have been made to the-- to the non-Jewish world, to the world generally, to realize how-- how low man can stoop, how animal-- how he becomes such an animal. Men of culture, men of PhDs, doctors, scientists, they can turn over just like that in 24 hours from a wonderful human being to an animal, worse than  an animal. And that's the message. And of course, thank God we got Israel. We wouldn't have got Israel without the Holocaust. Something forced it. And even the non-Jewish world would have realized-- the United Nations realized it. Something went wrong. And we've had this terrible calamity with the assassination of Rabbi [INAUDIBLE]. We don't know what's going to happen. But we must be-- we, ourselves, be prepared. It should never happen to us. We fight and die for it rather than give in to these [INAUDIBLE]. And we're glad to hear that many of the non-- non-Jewish schools, [INAUDIBLE] organizations are beginning to feel the trauma of the Holocaust. And they're being prepared to give their might and mane to ensure that it doesn't happen again. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That's it. Amen and amen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Would you like to introduce your wife for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, this is my wife, Josi. We've been married 50--

speaker: interviewee
text: 59 years.

speaker: interviewee
text: --59 a bit years. Please God, we'll have been married 60 years October the 14th of 1996. And she's been my helpmate. And I couldn't have done it without her. She doesn't prepare my sermons. That's the only snag of difficulty I've got. But she's a [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], a woman of worth. And when I'm wrong, she shouts at me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rightly so.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- But thank God. May she live long.

speaker: interviewer
text: When your husband came back after the war and he'd been to Belsen and helped the people, did you see a change in him? How did he cope when he came home?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a wee bit harder in his attitude. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] How can I put it? He was-- at one time, he'd give a soft answer if I shouted at him. [LAUGHS] Here, he would raise his voice. Of course, we put it down. I-- I didn't argue about it, because we put it down to shock that when he started telling us what happened in the camps there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he talked about it as soon as he came back?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, quite a lot. And I think it was the best thing, because if he bottled it up, it would have been dreadful.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Well, I suppose you found it quite unbelievable.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it was horrifying.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Just-- just couldn't-- couldn't imagine anything like that happening. It's like our latest case in London here. It's just incredible that things like that can happen. But we're still-- we're all right. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right? [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Reverend Hardman, would you like to explain this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. This is a quotation from the Book of Ezekiel. [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]

speaker: interviewer
text: 'Will these bones relive?' You know, it's the valley of the dry bones, you remember. And it's a quotation. 'Will these-- will these bones live?' As if to say-- that's what I asked when I came in. And it's got Rabbi Leslie Hardman, liberator, healer, mentor, and friend. To [INAUDIBLE], those remain-- remaining of the-- of those who-- who-- who were delivered. Bergen-Belsen, 1945. Presented by the Simon Wiesenthal Center the 30th of this [INAUDIBLE] 5755 [INAUDIBLE], April the 30th, 1995. Correct?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? INT: ?] [? Longer. ?] Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you ready?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to tell us about this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, this was the platform the British army made On which the Colonel [? Bird ?] and Brigadier [? Hughes, ?] and Colonel Taylor-- I can't recognize the other fellow-- about to speak to the assembly. That was the older troops. Before they-- they burned down the-- the filthy huts. You see it in the background. It's Colonel [? Bird ?] speaks before the camp is burned down.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and the people who were in the camp had been moved away by then?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were. Yes, yes. Interestingly enough, beyond the-- those-- those-- those huts, there were the gypsies, non-Jewish gypsies. And they suffered terribly, too. And they didn't want to move, just didn't want to move. And the only way they could be forced to move is as soon as the flight-- the flamethrowers went-- went-- went over these huts, and then they run out. Yes, that was the final destruction of Belsen. And today, it's one of the most beautiful parks. You wouldn't recognize it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Recording.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a scene in the-- in camp number two after the camp number one had been burned down and all the people were transferred to this camp number two. We held the-- a Pentecost service in the open, as you can see. And by that time, I had two colleagues coming in. Both were-- were prisoners of war. The one on the right, Rabbi [? Goldfinger, ?] was in the Polish army. And there's Rabbi [? Helfgott ?] of the Yugoslav army. And they helped.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they conducted part of the service, the davening itself. And I-- we had a Sefer Torah, so I did the reading of the Law. And interestingly enough, I'd come back to England. I'd already established in Hendon. And this picture was sent to me with a circle around the face or head of one of these girls-- begging of me to let them know, is she still alive and where is she? Unfortunately, I wouldn't know. I wouldn't know any of them, not even their names. And you see, most of them were women. The-- very, very few of the men came.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you get the Sefer from for the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Interestingly enough, it was a man who was the owner of this house before we came. He was a member of Hendon Shul. He-- no, no, no, I brought it from-- I'm sorry. I brought it from England-- this very-- very small one. He made-- he made--

speaker: interviewer
text: The cover?

speaker: interviewee
text: He made the ark for it--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, right.

speaker: interviewee
text: --the box for it, yeah, the [HEBREW]. Yes, he made it. And then, obviously, I left it there when I left. And that's right. Yes. Interesting enough, I remember-- not the Secret Service-- some section of the-- of the British army. A couple of officers came to me-- we'd like you to come. We've just discovered certain articles, which I think are Jewish. And they took me down into a cellar, and they found three Sefer Torah-- very huge-- their largest-- one of them was the large-- largest one I'd ever seen.

speaker: interviewer
text: The colonel got to know of it-- non-Jewish colonel. And he wanted it as a-- as-- as a gift for the army. You know? I said, I'm sorry, I said, they're too precious. I think that-- we hope that the Jewish community from which they were taken will rebuild itself, and they'll need it, because it takes years before one of these things are-- are-- are completed. And it costs a lot of money. So I've got a feeling he agreed with me. He didn't want it as a spoil of war. He wanted it originally, but I managed to dissuade him from it. And-- so that is what that picture shows. [INAUDIBLE]. Could you explain this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was anticipated-- he fell in love with her. She was an inmate of [INAUDIBLE] Belsen. And whenever he was there-- I don't know how long he was there-- a week or two. And every second of the day, he was with her. So she was of the first ones who got on her feet very well. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: She has long hair here. How long was this after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Under a year. Must be under a year. Yes, yes. Yes. Must've been-- whether she-- whether it's something, I don't know. I don't inquire about that aspect of it all.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. But I mean, it was just noticeable to me that--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, she wasn't bald when we came in. No, no, no. No, no. No, that I remember. But whether she had that kind of hair, I [? doubt it. ?] But it grew. And then that was--

speaker: interviewer
text: So these are all English soldiers?

speaker: interviewee
text: All English, yes. And he was the-- he's the captain, the chief of the unit. So the-- it was decided to-- to go to Lubeck, Schleswig-Holstein. And she thought that the [INAUDIBLE] unfortunately. She realized the meaning of it. So when we-- I can't remember whether I took her there in my truck or he sent-- I'm sure I took her in my-- I couldn't bring her mother. That was a heartbreak.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which Jewish mother doesn't want to be at the chuppah of the child, especially having lost so many kids? And, so anyhow-- so that was the-- that was the-- they married in the synagogue there, the Lubeck Synagogue. Yes. Everybody chummed together, brought in food, and brought her flowers. And I believe that dress was made up from a parachute. He got a parachute from one of the fliers of the airmen. And that's it. OK, would you like to tell us about this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a photograph of me speaking to the president of the-- what's the name? Oh, I can't remember. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] T-- TCA.

speaker: interviewer
text: Trans--

speaker: interviewee
text: Trans--

speaker: interviewer
text: --Canada Airlines.

speaker: interviewee
text: --Canada Air-- Air-- that's right. He's the president of it. Now, he was the officer commanding of the Canadian unit who gave me so much food and medication, so as I mentioned in my speech before. And I'd lost track of them. I couldn't remember even his name. So when I arrived-- this was-- is it '62? Maybe '62 when I came to-- to-- to Canada. Oh, yes, I'd won a traveling scholarship. The United Synagogue awarded me a traveling scholarship. And so I decided to go to Canada and to America to discuss some aspects of the rabbinate there. And-- so my sister was living in Montreal, so I stayed with her. And I told the story. And the-- one of the journalists goes, oh, yes, Dusty [? Vimberg. ?] She got so excited. Said, this is a real human story. I'm going to find out who it is. And she found him. And he-- his office were 32 stories up. A tremendous building the TCA had.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his private sector, his PRO, came to me, [INAUDIBLE] you're an answer to a maiden's prayer. I said, what do you mean? He said, we've been in trouble-- or Mr. [? MacGregor ?] has been in trouble. In which way? He said, you know, the French MPs feel that they're let down, they're second-- second-class citizens.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when they go on one of his planes, he-- they deserve to be treated like an ordinary MP and, therefore, should be given first class. Something happened and he wasn't given the first class. So they'd been starting trouble. And-- so when I start to praise him, the PRO said, this is a wonderful answer to all these-- these problems. Interestingly enough, he opened a drawer. He said, I haven't opened this for years. And a big desk. And he took out his logbook. And there, it showed that I was there in the-- in the camp. And when I wrote about these things, I thought about a million cigarettes the other officers-- the other officer I met also, he said, 3 million we gave you. Yes. Well, there you are. I don't know whether that choked a lot of people. However, so that was the situation of that particular [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] photograph, of the story behind it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to tell us about this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Mrs. Ester [? Kline ?] hadn't been too well, so I went to visit her. Her husband, now [INAUDIBLE], too, was a senior journalist of And she was an expert in this kind of drawing. And I must've-- oh, yes, I know. I must have been to a wedding. And while she was living not far from the hotel where we were-- where we were-- where we were eating, I went over to see her, and I was in my evening wear. And she said, I'd love you to-- I'd love to make a photograph of you, a portrait of you. So she did it, there and then.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did it take her?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I don't know how long. I got it within two or three days.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, right. So she did a rough sketch and then did it [? from there? ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. Yes. I'll never forget. Lady [? Jacobovich ?] came to see it also. And when she saw this, she said, I want you to make one of me. [LAUGHS] So I was-- I was-- well, I was there to help others, too. Yeah. Mm. Two, three, four, five.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us about this one, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This is this year-- 1950-- 1955, where I was honored. That-- the lady is Mrs. Sugihara the widow now of Mr. Sugihara, the-- the foreign council-- the council general of-- of-- of the Japanese government living in Kovno, Lithuania. And night after night, day after day, he never slept for days and nights, writing out visas for hundreds of people-- Jews to go to Japan to find-- find refuge. He suffered a great deal. But nevertheless, he did it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I remember when his government ordered him to stop, he said to his wife, what's going to happen? Said, you go on. I believe it's God's command that you should continue to do it. And so they ordered him. And that's Mr. Spielberg. He was the-- one of the-- the hosts at that particular function.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was interesting-- I don't know whether I mentioned it when I talked to you before-- that I helped him in-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in completing the film, And-- so he was happy to do it. He was happy to shake hands with me. And remind-- and-- and he was glad to be reminded of my help. So that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I'm glad to-- to be-- to be able to show people that I'm-- I'm next door to Mr. Spielberg, at least in-- in body. She's a darling woman, Mrs. Sugihara. She's written a-- I think a film is being made out of her. I think it is. I'm not sure. A book could be written about her. Well, that's it, my dear.
